Investor Anatomy
US Credit Conditions
Home
US Credit Conditions
Integrates credit spreads, systemic-stress indicators, and private-credit growth signals to assess the prevailing US credit-conditions regime.

Regime Assessment:

  • Expansionary / Risk-Positive
  • High Confidence
  • Stable

Why This Regime:

  • The dominant drivers are the Credit Spreads Signal (1) and the Financial Stress Composite signal (2). Both carry high aggregation weight, high interpretation confidence, and high conviction with no internal conflict.
  • The Credit Spreads Signal is in a NORMAL regime, having transitioned from an EASY state. This indicates stabilization in corporate funding conditions (1). The Financial Stress Composite is in a Neutral regime and trends towards easing. This suggests supportive financial conditions (2). Both explicitly describe a mid-cycle environment, not yet exhibiting signs of late-cycle tightening.
  • The Financial Stress Index signal (3), a medium-weight tactical indicator, also shows Neutral conditions, reinforcing the stable and benign credit backdrop.
  • Downweighted signals include the Credit Conditions Signal (4) and the Private-Credit Impulse signal (5). The Credit Conditions Signal was downweighted due to its low aggregation weight, mixed interpretation confidence, and an internal conflict between easing spreads and slightly rising VIX (4). The Private-Credit Impulse signal was heavily downweighted due to low aggregation weight, low interpretation confidence, and incomplete data for its latest observation, creating internal conflict (5).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Strong reinforcement exists across the highest-weighted signals. The NORMAL regime of the Credit Spreads Signal (1) aligns directly with the Neutral and easing trend of the Financial Stress Composite (2). Both underscore stable, supportive, and mid-cycle credit conditions. The Financial Stress Index also supports a Neutral state (3).
  • Tensions are noted within the lower-weighted Credit Conditions Signal, where easing corporate spreads (HY and BBB) are partially offset by rising equity volatility (VIX) (4).
  • These tensions do not overturn the regime call. The dominant, high-weight signals are coherent, exhibit high confidence, and report no internal conflicts, providing a robust overall assessment.

Scenario Balance:

  • The dominant scenario projects continued stable and supportive credit conditions, consistent with an expansionary environment. This is driven by contained spreads and low financial stress.
  • The primary upside risk is a transition to further easing conditions. This would be triggered by sustained narrowing of credit spreads or a continued steepening of the yield curve (2).
  • The primary downside risk is a shift towards tightening credit or higher stress. This could be prompted by a material widening of high-yield spreads or a significant rise in systemic financial stress (1)(2).

What Would Change the Regime:

  • A rise in the High Yield Option-Adjusted Spread exceeding 50 basis points over three months, or the Credit Spreads Composite z-score reaching +0.5 or higher, would signal a shift to a Tightening regime (1).
  • A significant increase in high-yield option-adjusted spreads, such as the HY_OAS z-score exceeding +0.75, would invalidate the current easing stress signal (2).
  • The St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index (STLFSI4) rising above 0, or its robust z-score exceeding +0.5, would signal a shift to Bearish (risk-off) conditions (3).
Inputs
Credit Spreads Signal

Signal Summary:

  • The Credit Spreads Composite (CSC) (1) is currently in a NORMAL regime as of 2025-12-31, transitioning from an EASY regime in the prior month. This indicates a stabilization of corporate funding conditions towards a neutral state.
  • Conviction Band: High | Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence | Internal Conflict Flag: No.

Key Dynamics:

  • The latest High Yield (HY) OAS is 2.92% and Investment Grade (IG) OAS is 0.82%, both remaining at relatively low levels.
  • The composite z-score (CSC_z) stands at -0.416, moving out of the "EASY" threshold (<-0.5) but still well below "TIGHTENING" (>= +0.5).
  • Momentum is mixed, with 3-month annualized HY spread change showing slight expansion (z-score 0.108) while IG momentum is contracting (z-score -0.243).
  • Conditional Invalidation: A rise in HY OAS greater than 50 basis points in 3 months, or the CSC z-score increasing to +0.5 or higher, would shift the regime to TIGHTENING.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base Case dominant: Credit conditions remain NORMAL, with spreads contained. This is supported by the CSC_z within the neutral range and spreads below stress thresholds.
  • Upside secondary: Spreads widen, signaling TIGHTENING conditions. This could be triggered by an abrupt increase in HY spreads or a significant rise in the composite z-score.
  • Downside residual: Spreads tighten further, moving back to an EASY regime. This would occur if the CSC z-score drops below -0.5 while HY OAS remains below 3.5%.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months). Credit spreads reflect near-to-medium-term funding dynamics and market sentiment.
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: High. This signal is a critical gauge of corporate financial health and systemic risk, heavily influencing broader macro assessments.

Macro Relevance:

  • This signal primarily informs macro dimensions of Credit and Risk Sentiment.
  • Current conditions suggest a mid-cycle environment where credit access is neither overly easy nor constrained.
  • Typically, rising credit spreads precede equity market drawdowns and slower capital expenditure (1). It interacts with liquidity and volatility signals, such as the Financial Stress Index (2).

Data & References:

  • ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread (HY_OAS): 2.92% as of 2025-12-31.
  • ICE BofA US Corporate Master Option-Adjusted Spread (IG_OAS): 0.82% as of 2025-12-31.
  • The CSC_z value of -0.416 for 2025-12-31 is most influential for the current NORMAL regime classification.
  • Additional public datasets that would improve depth or reliability include the St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index (STLFSI4) (2) and the VIX Market Volatility signal (4) for cross-validation of financial stress.
Financial Stress Composite Signal

Signal Summary:

  • The Financial Stress Composite signal is currently in a Neutral regime, with the composite value at -0.591409 as of 2026-01-31 (1). This indicates stable, normal functioning within credit and funding markets, trending towards easing conditions.
  • Conviction Band: High | Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence | Internal Conflict Flag: No.

Key Dynamics:

  • The neutral state is driven by consistent low stress readings across all components for 2026-01-31: the St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index (Z_STL = -0.407309), high-yield option-adjusted spreads (Z_HY = -0.604177), and the inverted 10Y-2Y Treasury yield spread (Z_INV = -0.762742) (1).
  • Momentum indicates easing stress, with the composite value decreasing from -0.285203 in October 2025.
  • No material internal offsets; all components contribute to the low-stress interpretation.
  • Conditional Invalidation: A significant increase in high-yield option-adjusted spreads (e.g., HY_OAS z-score exceeding +0.75) would invalidate this easing stress signal.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base Case dominant: Continued Neutral regime, with a bias towards further easing conditions as indicated by recent trends in the composite.
  • Upside secondary: A transition to a Low_Stress regime, triggered by sustained narrowing of credit spreads or a further steepening of the yield curve.
  • Downside residual: A shift towards a High_Stress regime, prompted by a material widening of credit spreads or increased systemic financial stress.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months), reflecting the monthly frequency of input data and the pace of financial condition changes.
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: High, as financial stress is a fundamental macro condition with broad implications across asset classes.

Macro Relevance:

  • This signal primarily informs U.S. credit conditions and overall financial stability, indicating liquidity and risk sentiment.
  • It suggests a mid-cycle environment where financial conditions remain supportive, not yet exhibiting signs of late-cycle tightening.
  • Typically, declining financial stress is bullish for risk assets and can precede broader economic expansion, while rising stress often correlates with a flight to safety.

Data & References:

  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index (STLFSI4), ICE BofA U.S. High-Yield Option-Adjusted Spread (HY_OAS), 10-Year minus 2-Year Treasury Yield Spread (T10Y2Y), all as of 2026-01-31 (1).
  • The Z-scores for STLFSI4 (-0.407309), HY_OAS (-0.604177), and inverted T10Y2Y (-0.762742) were most influential (1).
  • Additional public datasets that would improve depth or reliability include the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) and Investment Grade corporate bond spreads.
Credit Conditions Signal

Signal Summary:

  • The Credit Conditions Signal is currently in a Neutral regime as of 2026-01-31, reflecting a stable funding backdrop with spreads and volatility near typical ranges (1).
  • Conviction Band: Low, Interpretation Confidence: Mixed Signals, Internal Conflict Flag: Yes.

Key Dynamics:

  • The composite `Credit_Conditions` z-score of -0.381933 falls within the Neutral band (1).
  • While high-yield (Z_HY: -0.586807) and BBB (Z_BBB: -0.660726) corporate bond spreads indicate easing pressure, the VIX (Z_VIX: 0.101174) component shows slightly rising equity volatility.
  • This divergence presents an internal offset, preventing a clear Easing classification despite compressed credit spreads (1).
  • Conditional Invalidation: A sustained rise in the composite z-score above 0.75, driven by a material widening of corporate spreads and elevated VIX levels, would signal a shift to a Tightening regime.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base Case dominant: Credit conditions are likely to remain Neutral, as easing in corporate spreads is balanced by underlying equity volatility.
  • Upside secondary: Further easing would require a consistent decline in equity volatility alongside continued corporate spread compression.
  • Downside secondary: A significant and sustained widening of high-yield and BBB spreads could push the signal into a Tightening regime.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months). The signal incorporates monthly data and a 3-month exponential moving average, indicating a focus on medium-term funding stress trends (1).
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Low. Given the mixed underlying signals and low conviction, this signal should be weighted cautiously in broader macro composites.

Macro Relevance:

  • This signal primarily informs the liquidity and financial stress dimensions of the macro environment (1)(2).
  • It is particularly relevant during mid-to-late cycle stages where funding conditions can impact economic momentum.
  • It typically interacts with other macro signals by influencing risk appetite, informing asset allocation decisions, and signaling potential economic inflection points.

Data & References:

  • ICE BofA US High Yield Option-Adjusted Spread (HY_OAS), latest as of 2026-01-31.
  • ICE BofA US BBB Option-Adjusted Spread (BBB_OAS), latest as of 2026-01-31.
  • CBOE Volatility Index (VIXCLS), latest as of 2026-01-31.
  • The z-scores for HY_OAS (-0.586807) and BBB_OAS (-0.660726) contribute to the easing bias, while the VIXCLS z-score (0.101174) provides a counteracting stress input.
  • Additional public datasets that would improve depth or reliability include the Federal Reserve Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey (SLOOS) and the St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index (3).
Private Credit Impulse Signal

Signal Summary:

  • The Private-Credit Impulse signal for January 2026 is at 0.644770, classifying it as "Stable" (1). This indicates steady credit growth, currently close to the threshold for accelerating conditions.
  • Conviction Band: Low | Interpretation Confidence: Low Confidence | Internal Conflict Flag: Yes.

Key Dynamics:

  • The current "Stable" regime is driven by the six-month annualised growth in Total Bank Credit, with its Z-score at 0.644770 for January 2026 (1).
  • However, the Z-scores for Business Loans and Total Consumer Loans are not available for this period, meaning the latest impulse is based on a single component (1).
  • This lack of complete data creates internal tension and significantly impacts confidence in the aggregate signal's reading.
  • Conditional Invalidation: The signal would reverse to "Decelerating" if the composite value falls below -0.75 (1).

Scenario Balance:

  • Base Case dominant: The Private-Credit Impulse remains "Stable", reflecting moderate and consistent credit expansion.
  • Upside secondary: Credit impulse accelerates beyond 0.75, driven by a broad pickup in business or consumer lending.
  • Downside residual: Credit impulse decelerates below -0.75, triggered by tightening financial conditions or a sharp drop in loan demand.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months), given its reliance on six-month annualised growth rates (1).
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Low, due to the low confidence stemming from incomplete data for the latest observation.

Macro Relevance:

  • This signal informs on liquidity and demand conditions, acting as a leading indicator for economic slowdowns (1).
  • It typically signals a mid-cycle position when stable, with acceleration suggesting early-cycle expansion.
  • The impulse interacts with growth and inflation signals, impacting investment and consumption trends.

Data & References:

  • Total Bank Credit (FRED: TOTBKCR), Commercial & Industrial Loans (FRED: BUSLOANS), Total Consumer Loans (FRED: TOTALSL).
  • Latest observation date: 2026-01-31 (1).
  • The most influential datapoint is the Z-score of six-month annualised Total Bank Credit growth at 0.644770 (1).
  • Adding credit demand surveys or bank lending officer surveys would improve depth.
Financial Stress Index Signal

Signal Summary:

  • The Financial Stress Index signal indicates Neutral conditions as of 2026-01-23, following recent oscillations.
  • Financial stress is currently stable, neither significantly tightening nor easing, after a brief period of elevated stress in late 2025.
  • Conviction Band: Medium | Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence | Internal Conflict Flag: No

Key Dynamics:

  • The STLFSI4 registered -0.7123, with its robust z-score at -0.364431 as of 2026-01-23.
  • The current state is driven by the z-score remaining within the +/-0.5 threshold for Neutral classification (1).
  • Momentum has been oscillatory, transitioning from Bearish in November 2025 to Neutral, then Bullish, before settling back into Neutral.
  • Conditional Invalidation: The signal would reverse to Bearish (risk-off) if the STLFSI4 value exceeds 0 or its robust z-score rises above +0.5 (1).

Scenario Balance:

  • Base Case dominant: Financial conditions remain neutral, reflecting a lack of extreme stress or ease.
  • Upside secondary: A sustained decline in the z-score below -0.5, alongside negative STLFSI4, could trigger a Bullish (risk-on) signal.
  • Downside residual: A notable increase in the STLFSI4 above 0 or its z-score above +0.5 would signal renewed Bearish (risk-off) conditions.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Tactical (weeks), as the signal updates weekly and reflects immediate shifts in financial conditions.
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium, given its direct relevance to market sentiment and financial conditions, but it benefits from integration with other macro signals.

Macro Relevance:

  • Macro dimension informed: Financial stress, market sentiment, and risk-on/risk-off dynamics (1).
  • Cycle position: A Neutral reading typically suggests stable or mid-cycle conditions, rather than extreme late-cycle stress or early-cycle ease.
  • Typical interaction with other macro signals: Often combined with credit spreads (2), market volatility (VIX) (3), and liquidity indicators (4) to provide a comprehensive macro regime view (5).

Data & References:

  • STLFSI4 (St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index), latest observation 2026-01-23.
  • The STLFSI4 value of -0.7123 and its robust z-score of -0.364431 were most influential (1).
  • Additional public datasets that would improve depth or reliability include the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Option-Adjusted Spread (FRED: BAMLH0A0HYM2) and the CBOE Volatility Index (FRED: VIXCLS).
Investor Anatomy • US Credit Conditions • Not Investment Advice
